serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 1 whole small fish or portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Crispy Fried Egg Fish is approximately 150g, representing one whole small fish or a generous portion, commonly served alongside steamed rice as part of a Vietnamese family meal.

nutrient_source.title: Cá Trứng Chiên Giòn — Crispy Fried Egg Fish
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crispy Fried Egg Fish (Cá Trứng Chiên Giòn), cr_id G00010579, with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, sodium, dietary fibre, sugar) estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogous entry for fried fish (SR Legacy #15264 — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) and adjusted for deep-frying preparation. fat_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB database entry and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish and seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate fried fish
gi_source.source_match_notes: Crispy Fried Egg Fish contains only 2.36g carbohydrate per 100g with negligible sugar. No direct GI measurement exists for this dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 40 (low category) is assigned as an analogous estimate based on comparable low-carbohydrate fried fish preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently fall in the low GI range due to minimal digestible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (40) × carbohydrate per serving (3.54g from 2.36g/100g × 150g) / 100 = 1.4, which is clinically negligible. This estimate is conservative and appropriate given the very low carbohydrate content of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and salt marinade estimated at approximately 350mg per 100g; deep-frying preparation may further concentrate sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100g or less, request reduced fish sauce and salt in the marinade, avoid additional dipping sauces, and prefer grilled or steamed preparation to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is very low at 2.36g per 100g with negligible sugar; high protein at 10.7g per 100g from fish supports satiety and blood glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Crispy Fried Egg Fish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load; however, patients should monitor total meal carbohydrate load from accompanying rice and avoid batter-coated preparations that add refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying preparation increases total fat and saturated fat content; however, the HPB-reported fat value of 0.0g/100g suggests minimal fat absorption in this specific preparation, and fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer grilled or steamed egg fish to preserve omega-3 fatty acid benefits and avoid the pro-inflammatory effects of deep-frying; if consuming the fried version, limit portion to 100–150g and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fried Egg Fish (Cá Trứng Chiên Giòn) is a Vietnamese dish featuring egg fish — a small, roe-bearing fish species — that is marinated in a blend of fish sauce, salt, garlic, and turmeric, then deep-fried until the exterior becomes golden and crispy while the interior remains tender and flaky. The dish is a staple of Vietnamese home cooking and is commonly served alongside steamed jasmine rice, pickled vegetables, and dipping sauces as part of a family meal at lunch or dinner. The name 'cá trứng' literally translates to 'egg fish,' referring to the fish's roe-filled belly, which becomes particularly rich and flavourful when fried. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein at 10.7g per 100g and very low in carbohydrates at 2.36g per 100g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The HPB Singapore database records fat at 0.0g per 100g for this preparation, which may reflect the specific fish variety, cooking method, or database measurement methodology. Sodium content is estimated at approximately 350mg per 100g from the marinade. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal dietary requirements. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycaemic impact (GI approximately 40, GL approximately 1.4 per 150g serving), but individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should be mindful of sodium from marinades and the pro-inflammatory effects of deep-frying.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010579) with confidence 0.95 and reviewed provenance. The HPB entry is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Secondary nutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous fried fish entry and documented accordingly. GI assigned as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference class for low-carbohydrate fried fish.
notes: fat_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010579, confidence 0.95); this is confirmed zero per the approved primary source. This is an unusual value for a deep-fried preparation and may reflect the specific fish variety, cooking method, or database measurement methodology. fat_g confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g set to 0.0 consistent with the HPB-reported fat_g of 0.0. sugar_g set to 0.0 as no sugar is present in the marinade or fish; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central analogous fried fish data. dietary_fibre_g set to 0.0 as fish contains no dietary fibre; naturally zero for this food type. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile data for this specific Vietnamese crispy fried egg fish preparation not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. sodium_mg estimated at 350mg per 100g based on typical Vietnamese fish marinade composition (fish sauce, salt) using USDA FoodData Central analogous data; HPB source does not report sodium for this entry. Glycemic index assigned as 40 (low category) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference class for low-carbohydrate fried fish preparations; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. Glycemic load per 150g serving = 1.4, clinically negligible. The health_suitability data from the input record has been reviewed; the hypertension sodium estimate of ~600mg/100g from the input has been revised to 350mg/100g based on USDA analogous data, as 600mg/100g is not supported by available approved sources for this preparation.
